19	 
20	 ## Rollback Playbook
21	 
22	-_To be authored in Story 4.1 — rollback playbook covering scenarios such as failed publish, tag mismatch, bad `latest` dist-tag, OIDC auth failure, and post-publish security issue._
23	+> **NEVER `npm unpublish` v1.0.0.** Once `bmad-module-skill-forge` is published under `--tag latest` at v1.0.0, the version is immutable by policy (NFR6). The default rollback is `npm deprecate` + ship forward. See Scenario C for the narrow 72h / zero-dependents exception — which applies to **pre-v1.0.0 versions only**.
24	+
25	+Scenarios A–F are recovery paths for a bad publish; Scenario G is a meta-recovery path for the release workflow itself. Each scenario follows a five-element shape: **Trigger** → **CLI** → **Expected outcome** → **Constraints** → **Verification**. A compact [cross-reference matrix](#cross-reference-matrix) sits at the end of this section for under-pressure triage. All `gh api` examples in this section use name-based lookups (ruleset by `name=="Default"`, environment by literal `release`) so they survive ruleset or environment re-creation.
26	+
27	+Placeholder substitutions used throughout:
28	+
29	+- `<bad>` — the broken version just published (e.g. `0.10.1`).
30	+- `<previous_good>` — the last known-good version immediately before `<bad>` (e.g. `0.10.0`).
31	+- `<next_version>` — the fix version produced by the next `release.yaml` dispatch (e.g. `0.10.2`).
32	+- `<run-id>` — a GitHub Actions run id visible in the Actions UI and via `gh run list --workflow=release.yaml`.
33	+
34	+### Scenario A — "Bad version published to `latest`, no users yet (within ~minutes)"
35	+
36	+- **Trigger.** Maintainer notices a defect within minutes of publish; `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge dist-tags` still shows `<bad>` on `latest`; download count is negligible; no dependents have pinned `<bad>` yet.
37	+- **CLI.**
38	+
39	+  ```bash
40	+  # Flip latest back to the last known-good version.
41	+  npm dist-tag add bmad-module-skill-forge@<previous_good> latest
42	+
43	+  # Verify the flip landed.
44	+  npm dist-tag ls bmad-module-skill-forge
45	+
46	+  # Warn anyone who did install <bad> in the meantime.
47	+  npm deprecate bmad-module-skill-forge@<bad> "Pulled - use <previous_good> instead"
48	+
49	+  # Cut the fix forward via the canonical workflow (OIDC publish, SLSA-L2 provenance).
50	+  gh workflow run release.yaml -f version_bump=patch
51	+  ```
52	+
53	+- **Expected outcome.** `latest` now resolves to `<previous_good>`; `npm install bmad-module-skill-forge@<bad>` emits a deprecation warning; the subsequent patch cut advances `latest` to `<next_version>` cleanly.
54	+- **Constraints.** Valid only while the blast radius is small. If any downstream has already pinned to `<bad>`, Scenario B applies instead — reverting `latest` won't reach those installs.
55	+- **Verification.**
56	+
57	+  ```bash
58	+  npm view bmad-module-skill-forge dist-tags --json
59	+  # expected: {"latest":"<previous_good>", ... "alpha":"0.10.1-alpha.0"}
60	+  ```
61	+
62	+### Scenario B — "Bad version live for hours/days, users may be installing it"
63	+
64	+- **Trigger.** Defect discovered after `<bad>` has held `latest` long enough that download count is non-zero, or dependents may have already pinned.
65	+- **CLI.**
66	+
67	+  ```bash
68	+  # Warn immediately — every install of <bad> now prints this string.
69	+  npm deprecate bmad-module-skill-forge@<bad> "Critical bug - use <next_version> instead"
70	+
71	+  # Cut the fix forward. release.yaml's dist-tag case-chain auto-updates `latest`
72	+  # for non-prerelease versions, so no manual `dist-tag add` is needed here.
73	+  gh workflow run release.yaml -f version_bump=patch
74	+  ```
75	+
76	+- **Expected outcome.** `npm install bmad-module-skill-forge` starts emitting the deprecation warning; the new patch publishes cleanly; `latest` advances to `<next_version>`.
77	+- **Constraints.** **Do NOT** `npm dist-tag add @<previous_good> latest` in this scenario. For post-v1.0.0 releases that is an NFR6 violation (it re-exposes a deprecated version as `latest`); for pre-v1.0.0 it fragments user expectation and hides the fix. Ship forward via patch bump.
78	+- **Verification.**
79	+
80	+  ```bash
81	+  # Deprecation warning appears on a fresh install.
82	+  npm install bmad-module-skill-forge@<bad> 2>&1 | grep -i deprecat
83	+  ```
84	+
85	+  NFR3 (rollback frequency <1/quarter post-v1.0.0) is evaluated against the frequency of Scenario B invocations — each trip through Scenario B is an NFR3 data point.
86	+
87	+### Scenario C — "Bad version within last 72h, zero downloads, zero dependents"
88	+
89	+- **Trigger.** A narrow eligibility window per the [npm unpublish policy](https://docs.npmjs.com/policies/unpublish/): less than 72 hours since publish **AND** zero downloads **AND** zero dependent packages registered on npmjs.com. All three conditions must be true.
90	+- **CLI.**
91	+
92	+  ```bash
93	+  npm unpublish bmad-module-skill-forge@<bad>
94	+  ```
95	+
96	+- **Expected outcome.** The version disappears from `npm view`. **But** the version *number* is permanently burned — npm rejects any future publish of that same version string forever.
97	+
98	+  > **Permanent-burn warning.** The version number is burned. After `npm unpublish bmad-module-skill-forge@0.10.1`, the next `release.yaml` dispatch with `version_bump: patch` on `0.10.0` will produce `0.10.2`, **not** `0.10.1` again. Attempting `npm version 0.10.1 --no-git-tag-version` would drive the workflow's `npm publish` toward an npm-burned version and return `403`. Treat the unpublish as a one-way jump in the version number line, not a true undo.
99	+
100	+- **Constraints.** **This scenario does NOT apply to v1.0.0.** Per NFR6, v1.0.0 is never unpublished regardless of eligibility window — use Scenario B instead. Scenario C is a pre-v1.0.0-only path.
101	+- **Verification.**
102	+
103	+  ```bash
104	+  npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@<bad> 2>&1
105	+  # expected: npm error 404 No match found for version <bad>
106	+  ```
107	+
108	+### Scenario D — "Tag exists but npm publish failed"
109	+
110	+- **Trigger.** `release.yaml` pushed the `v*` tag (tag-creation step runs before the npm publish step) and then the publish step failed — OIDC 404, tarball validation error, network drop. `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@<version>` returns 404; `git tag -l v<version>` shows the tag locally and on origin.
111	+- **CLI.**
112	+
113	+  ```bash
114	+  # VERIFY FIRST that npm did NOT publish — a tag-delete after a successful
115	+  # publish would orphan the npm artifact. If this returns a manifest, STOP
116	+  # and go to Scenario E.
117	+  npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@<version> 2>&1
118	+
119	+  # Tag-only recovery: clear the tag locally and on origin, then re-dispatch.
120	+  git tag -d v<version>
121	+  git push --delete origin v<version>
122	+
123	+  # Re-dispatch with the same bump input — release.yaml will produce a fresh
124	+  # clean tag and successful publish.
125	+  gh workflow run release.yaml -f version_bump=<same-input-as-before>
126	+  ```
127	+
128	+- **Expected outcome.** Tag cleared from origin; re-run produces a fresh clean tag plus a successful publish under the next version number.
129	+- **Constraints.** Only safe if publish failed. If npm *did* publish, use Scenario E — tag-deletion after a successful publish leaves the npm artifact without a matching git ref.
130	+- **Do-NOT clause.** Never re-use the burned `<version>` number. `release.yaml` will produce the next version on redispatch (for example, re-running an `alpha` bump over `0.10.1-alpha.0` produces `0.10.1-alpha.1`, not `0.10.1-alpha.0` again). Forcing the original version back via `npm version <exact> --no-git-tag-version` is out of scope for rollback.
131	+- **Story 3.2 load-bearing context.** The current `release.yaml` pushes the git tag **before** the npm publish step (see the `Create and push tag` job step vs. the `Publish to npm via OIDC trusted publishing` step). This ordering is pre-existing from Story 3.1 and tracked in `_bmad-output/implementation-artifacts/deferred-work.md` under `§ 3-1/3-2 code review "Create and push tag pushes the git tag BEFORE Publish to npm"` as a post-v1.0.0 hardening candidate. Until that lands, Scenario D's tag-delete path **is** the recovery.
132	+- **Verification.**
133	+
134	+  ```bash
135	+  # Tag gone on origin.
136	+  gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/git/refs/tags/v<version> 2>&1
137	+  # expected: 404 Not Found
138	+  ```
139	+
140	+### Scenario E — "npm publish succeeded but GitHub Release / tag push failed"
141	+
142	+- **Trigger.** Publish succeeded (`npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@<version>` returns a manifest) but **one of**: tag-push to origin failed (network drop, branch-protection edge case); the `Create GitHub Release` softprops step failed (API rate limit, auth expiry); the `Push commit to main` step was (correctly) skipped but the release artifact diverged from `main`.
143	+- **Premise inversion note.** In the research doc's scenario ordering, publish was assumed to happen before tag push — so publish failure was the likely branch. In the live `release.yaml` the ordering is reversed: the tag push runs **before** the npm publish. That means a publish-succeeded-but-tag-missing window is small, but real — the `Create GitHub Release` and `Push commit to main` steps still run after publish and either can fail independently. This scenario covers the post-publish-tag-or-release-missing recovery path.
144	+- **CLI — tag recovery.**
145	+
146	+  ```bash
147	+  # Obtain the commit-sha the workflow used as its HEAD during the publish run.
148	+  gh run view <run-id> --log | grep -A 2 "release: bump to v<version>"
149	+
150	+  # Recreate the annotated tag and push.
151	+  git tag -a v<version> <commit-sha> -m "Release v<version>"
152	+  git push origin v<version>
153	+  ```
154	+
155	+- **CLI — GitHub Release recovery.**
156	+
157	+  ```bash
158	+  # Re-create the GitHub Release from the notes the workflow generated
159	+  # (scrape from the run log if release_notes.md was not preserved as an artifact).
160	+  gh release create v<version> \
161	+    --notes-file release_notes.md \
162	+    --title "Skill Forge (SKF) v<version>"
163	+  ```
164	+
165	+- **Expected outcome.** Tag landed on origin pointing at the correct commit; GitHub Release page reflects the published npm artifact; npm + GitHub + git state are now consistent.
166	+- **Constraints.** **npm state is immutable.** Do NOT try to "clean up" the npm artifact so the release can be re-run from scratch. The npm artifact plus the orphaned post-recovery git state **is** the canonical record — NFR5 (audit-trail completeness) is satisfied by the successful npm publish plus the recovered tag and GitHub Release, not by a clean rerun.
167	+- **Orphaned-commit caveat (Story 3.2 context).** If the run was dispatched from a feature branch and `Push commit to main` was correctly skipped, the `release: bump to v<version>` commit lives only in the workflow run log until the recovered tag above anchors it. This is NFR12-compliant and expected pre-v1.0.0 behavior; the tag is the authoritative pointer. This is exactly the pattern observed in the Story 3.2 alpha cut (`bmad-module-skill-forge@0.10.1-alpha.0`, run `24714953668`, tag `v0.10.1-alpha.0`, orphaned commit `2a57dcbd`).
168	+- **Verification.**
169	+
170	+  ```bash
171	+  # Tag resolves.
172	+  gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/git/refs/tags/v<version>
173	+
174	+  # GitHub Release exists.
175	+  gh release view v<version>
176	+
177	+  # npm + GitHub provenance still match.
178	+  npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@<version> --json | jq '.dist.attestations'
179	+  ```
180	+
181	+### Scenario F — "Suspected OIDC compromise / unauthorized publish"
182	+
183	+- **Trigger (any of).**
184	+  - `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge time` shows a publish with a timestamp no maintainer triggered.
185	+  - `npm audit signatures` on a published tarball reports attestation verification failure.
186	+  - A GitHub Actions run shows reviewer approval that was not given.
187	+  - npm support notifies of suspected compromise.
188	+- **Immediate CLI (within minutes of detection).** Revoke the OIDC trust surface before doing anything else:
189	+  - Visit `https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge` → **Settings** tab → **Trusted Publisher** section → **Delete** the registration.
190	+  - This blocks *all* future OIDC publishes until re-registered, stopping any further unauthorized use of the OIDC path mid-incident.
191	+- **Audit CLI.**
192	+
193	+  ```bash
194	+  # Full publish timeline.
195	+  npm view bmad-module-skill-forge time
196	+
197	+  # Per-version provenance shape (script-friendly).
198	+  npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@<suspect> --json \
199	+    | jq '.dist.attestations'
200	+  # expected shape:
201	+  # {
202	+  #   "url": "https://registry.npmjs.org/-/npm/v1/attestations/bmad-module-skill-forge@<suspect>",
203	+  #   "provenance": { "predicateType": "https://slsa.dev/provenance/v1" }
204	+  # }
205	+
206	+  # Human-friendly signature-chain check on a fresh install.
207	+  npm audit signatures
208	+  # expected: "X packages have verified attestations"
209	+  ```
210	+
211	+  If `dist.attestations` is missing or the `url` field points somewhere unexpected, the publish bypassed the OIDC path.
212	+
213	+- **Coordination CLI.** If a malicious version was actually published, contact `support@npmjs.com` with the version string, the attestation URL, and the Trusted Publisher registration timestamp; request a manual takedown. npm support can unpublish outside the 72-hour window for security-compromise cases.
214	+- **Lockdown CLI.**
215	+
216	+  ```bash
217	+  # Confirm no unexpected secrets were added to the repo.
218	+  gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/actions/secrets
219	+
220	+  # Confirm the write-access list is unchanged.
221	+  gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/collaborators
222	+
223	+  # Review all workflows for any pull_request trigger type that could run
224	+  # attacker-controlled code with repo secrets (e.g. `types: [opened]`
225	+  # without a trusted-author gate).
226	+  grep -rn "pull_request:" .github/workflows/
227	+  ```
228	+
229	+- **Post-incident reactivation.** After audit completes and root cause is identified and patched, re-register the Trusted Publisher via the npm UI with the four fields matching the table in `## npm Trusted Publisher` above (`organization=armelhbobdad`, `repository=bmad-module-skill-forge`, `workflow filename=release.yaml`, `environment=release`). Re-verify via an alpha cut (dispatch `release.yaml` from a temporarily-allowed feature branch per the `## Release Environment § Temporarily allowing a feature branch` procedure) before any stable release.
230	+- **Pre-v1.0.0 context.** Trusted Publisher was pre-registered on 2026-04-20 (Story 1.3). A compromise discovered pre-v1.0.0 is recoverable via delete-and-re-register with no downstream-consumer blast radius (no stable release users yet). Post-v1.0.0, the incident has downstream blast radius and the `support@npmjs.com` coordination path is load-bearing.
231	+- **Do-NOT clause — `NPM_TOKEN` rotation is not OIDC incident response.** Do NOT rotate `NPM_TOKEN` as a first response. The token is not on the OIDC path; rotating it does nothing to stop an OIDC compromise.
232	+  - If `NPM_TOKEN` itself is *also* suspected compromised, revoke via `https://www.npmjs.com` → **Access Tokens** and remove from the repo: `gh secret delete NPM_TOKEN --repo armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge`. This is separate from the OIDC incident response above.
233	+  - `NPM_TOKEN` still exists at repo scope until Story 6.3 (post-v1.0.0). A compromised `NPM_TOKEN` is a **separate incident class** from OIDC compromise. During the window where OIDC is revoked *and* `NPM_TOKEN` is also compromised, there is no valid publish path — the repo enters lockdown until Trusted Publisher is re-registered. Document the flip in the incident post-mortem; do not publish via any stale path.
234	+
235	+### Scenario G — "release.yaml disabled, reverted, or missing from main"
236	+
237	+- **Trigger.** `release.yaml` is the single-rooted release workflow (Story 3.3 Patch A neutralized the legacy `publish.yaml` `v*` tag trigger). If `release.yaml` is reverted, renamed, moved, or disabled at the repo-settings level (`gh workflow disable release.yaml`) and someone tries to cut a release, **no workflow fires**. Silent no-op until a maintainer checks `npm view` or the Actions UI.
238	+- **Detection.** After any PR that touches `.github/workflows/*` — and before any release attempt — run:
239	+
240	+  ```bash
241	+  gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/actions/workflows \
242	+    --jq '.workflows[] | select(.name=="Release") | {name, state, path}'
243	+  # expected: {"name":"Release","state":"active","path":".github/workflows/release.yaml"}
244	+  ```
245	+
246	+  If `state` is not `active` (npm reports `disabled_manually` when disabled via the UI or `gh workflow disable`), or no matching workflow is returned at all (file renamed, moved, or deleted), Scenario G applies.
247	+
248	+- **Recovery CLI.**
249	+
250	+  ```bash
251	+  # Case 1: workflow is disabled but the file is present.
252	+  gh workflow enable release.yaml
253	+
254	+  # Case 2: the file is missing or reverted.
255	+  # Revert the offending PR via the normal branch-protection review path.
256	+  # Do NOT hand-edit main — branch protection blocks direct push anyway.
257	+  gh pr view <offending-pr-number>
258	+  gh pr revert <offending-pr-number>
259	+  ```
260	+
261	+- **Expected outcome.** `release.yaml` is back on `main` with `state: "active"`; next dispatch fires normally.
262	+- **Prevention.** Single-root invariants to spot-check after any workflow-dir PR:
263	+
264	+  ```bash
265	+  # Any workflow with id-token: write should be a known release/provenance workflow.
266	+  grep -l 'id-token: write' .github/workflows/*.yaml
267	+  # expected set: docs.yaml (GitHub Pages), publish.yaml (DEPRECATED, workflow_dispatch-only),
268	+  # release.yaml (canonical).
269	+
270	+  # No v* push trigger outside release.yaml — and release.yaml itself is workflow_dispatch-only,
271	+  # so there should be zero matches for `v*` push triggers anywhere.
272	+  grep -rE 'tags:\s*$|v\*' .github/workflows/*.yaml
273	+  ```
274	+
275	+- **Escalation path — emergency hatch.** If Scenario G is detected mid-incident when a release is urgently needed, the legacy `.github/workflows/publish.yaml` retains `workflow_dispatch:` as an emergency hatch (NPM_TOKEN still at repo scope until Story 6.3). Invoke via:
276	+
277	+  ```bash
278	+  gh workflow run publish.yaml
279	+  ```
280	+
281	+  with the explicit understanding that this publish is **token-based (not OIDC)** and will **not** carry SLSA-L2 provenance. Document every emergency-hatch use in an incident post-mortem, and re-enable `release.yaml` before the next release. This fallback disappears when Story 6.1 deletes `publish.yaml`.
282	+
283	+- **Constraints.** Branch protection on `main` blocks direct pushes — recovery goes through a PR in every case. Do not attempt to sidestep branch protection to "fix" `release.yaml` faster; the cost of a bad release (NFR5 audit-trail breakage, NFR10 commit-trail breakage) far exceeds the cost of a normal-review PR.
284	+
285	+### Cross-reference matrix
286	+
287	+| Scenario | Trigger class                   | Primary CLI verb              | NFR linkage           |
288	+| -------- | ------------------------------- | ----------------------------- | --------------------- |
289	+| A        | Fresh bad latest                | `dist-tag` + `deprecate`      | NFR3                  |
290	+| B        | Stale bad latest                | `deprecate` + ship forward    | NFR3, NFR6            |
291	+| C        | Eligible unpublish              | `unpublish`                   | (pre-v1.0.0 only)     |
292	+| D        | Tag orphan (publish failed)     | `tag -d` + `push --delete`    | NFR12                 |
293	+| E        | Post-publish state drift        | `tag -a` + `gh release create`| NFR5                  |
294	+| F        | OIDC compromise                 | revoke Trusted Publisher + audit | NFR7               |
295	+| G        | `release.yaml` disabled/missing | `workflow enable` / `pr revert` | NFR5, NFR10         |
296	+
297	+### Baseline snapshots
298	+
299	+The ruleset-restore snippet at the top of this document (`## Branch Protection on main § Restore from a saved baseline`) uses `gh api --method PUT .../rulesets/<id>`. That call returns `404` if the `Default` ruleset has been deleted, not merely edited. Keeping a recent `baseline-ruleset-Default-YYYYMMDD.json` on disk lets the PUT-then-POST fallback below recover from either case.
300	+
301	+**One-time capture pattern** — run after any maintainer-initiated change to the ruleset or to the `release` environment, **not on a schedule**:
302	+
303	+```bash
304	+# Capture the current Default ruleset as a disaster-recovery baseline.
305	+# Uses name-based lookup so the snippet survives ruleset re-creation (IDs change, names don't).
306	+RULESET_ID=$(gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/rulesets \
307	+  --jq '.[] | select(.name=="Default") | .id')
308	+gh api "repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/rulesets/$RULESET_ID" \
309	+  > "_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/baseline-ruleset-Default-$(date +%Y%m%d).json"
310	+
311	+# Capture the release environment + its branch policy list (two separate resources).
312	+gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release \
313	+  > "_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/baseline-env-release-$(date +%Y%m%d).json"
314	+gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies \
315	+  > "_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/baseline-env-release-branch-policies-$(date +%Y%m%d).json"
316	+```
317	+
318	+Baselines live in `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/` (already git-tracked, not shipped with the npm package). The filename date stamp makes the freshness of the baseline obvious at a glance.
319	+
320	+**Ruleset-deletion recovery path — PUT-then-POST.** The canonical restore flow is:
321	+
322	+1. Try `PUT` first. If the ruleset exists but drifted, this reconciles it in place.
323	+
324	+   ```bash
325	+   RULESET_ID=$(gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/rulesets \
326	+     --jq '.[] | select(.name=="Default") | .id')
327	+   jq '{name, target, enforcement, conditions, rules, bypass_actors}' \
328	+     _bmad-output/planning-artifacts/baseline-ruleset-Default-YYYYMMDD.json \
329	+     > /tmp/restore.json
330	+   gh api --method PUT \
331	+     "repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/rulesets/$RULESET_ID" \
332	+     --input /tmp/restore.json
333	+   ```
334	+
335	+2. If the `PUT` returns `404` (ruleset was deleted, not edited), recreate via `POST`:
336	+
337	+   ```bash
338	+   gh api --method POST \
339	+     repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/rulesets \
340	+     --input /tmp/restore.json
341	+   ```
342	+
343	+The `POST` body needs the same `name`, `target`, `enforcement`, `conditions`, `rules`, and `bypass_actors` fields as the PUT — all captured by the baseline snapshot above. Try `PUT` first; on `404`, `POST` recreates with identical semantics. The ruleset gets a new id on re-creation (IDs are not stable across delete+create cycles); update any hard-coded id references in this document on the next planned edit pass.
344	+
345	+For the `release` environment, a deletion+restore similarly uses the two-call pattern already documented at `## Release Environment § Restore / re-apply` — feed the environment-level baseline JSON to the `PUT .../environments/release` call, then re-POST each entry from the branch-policies baseline to `.../environments/release/deployment-branch-policies`.
346

diff --git a/README.md b/README.md
index a562bcb..c908b8b 100644
--- a/README.md
+++ b/README.md
@@ -185,7 +185,7 @@ The v1.0.0 public API contract is documented in [docs/STABILITY.md](docs/STABILI
 
 ## Release Process
 
-Maintainers: see [docs/RELEASING.md](docs/RELEASING.md) for branch-protection rules, required status checks, the [`release` environment with required-reviewer gate](docs/RELEASING.md#release-environment), the [npm Trusted Publisher registration](docs/RELEASING.md#npm-trusted-publisher) (OIDC-backed publish, auto-provenance), and (later) the rollback playbook.
+Maintainers: see [docs/RELEASING.md](docs/RELEASING.md) for branch-protection rules, required status checks, the [`release` environment with required-reviewer gate](docs/RELEASING.md#release-environment), the [npm Trusted Publisher registration](docs/RELEASING.md#npm-trusted-publisher) (OIDC-backed publish, auto-provenance), and the [rollback playbook](docs/RELEASING.md#rollback-playbook) covering seven failure scenarios.
 
 ## License
 
diff --git a/docs/RELEASING.md b/docs/RELEASING.md
index 4bd0ec6..52a0c03 100644
--- a/docs/RELEASING.md
+++ b/docs/RELEASING.md
@@ -182,4 +182,326 @@ The future `release.yaml` workflow (Story 3.1) publishes to npm via **OIDC trust
 
 ## Rollback Playbook
 
-_To be authored in Story 4.1 — rollback playbook covering scenarios such as failed publish, tag mismatch, bad `latest` dist-tag, OIDC auth failure, and post-publish security issue._
+> **NEVER `npm unpublish` v1.0.0.** Once `bmad-module-skill-forge` is published under `--tag latest` at v1.0.0, the version is immutable by policy (NFR6). The default rollback is `npm deprecate` + ship forward. See Scenario C for the narrow 72h / zero-dependents exception — which applies to **pre-v1.0.0 versions only**.
+
+Scenarios A–F are recovery paths for a bad publish; Scenario G is a meta-recovery path for the release workflow itself. Each scenario follows a five-element shape: **Trigger** → **CLI** → **Expected outcome** → **Constraints** → **Verification**. A compact [cross-reference matrix](#cross-reference-matrix) sits at the end of this section for under-pressure triage. All `gh api` examples in this section use name-based lookups (ruleset by `name=="Default"`, environment by literal `release`) so they survive ruleset or environment re-creation.
+
+Placeholder substitutions used throughout:
+
+- `<bad>` — the broken version just published (e.g. `0.10.1`).
+- `<previous_good>` — the last known-good version immediately before `<bad>` (e.g. `0.10.0`).
+- `<next_version>` — the fix version produced by the next `release.yaml` dispatch (e.g. `0.10.2`).
+- `<run-id>` — a GitHub Actions run id visible in the Actions UI and via `gh run list --workflow=release.yaml`.
+
+### Scenario A — "Bad version published to `latest`, no users yet (within ~minutes)"
+
+- **Trigger.** Maintainer notices a defect within minutes of publish; `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge dist-tags` still shows `<bad>` on `latest`; download count is negligible; no dependents have pinned `<bad>` yet.
+- **CLI.**
+
+  ```bash
+  # Flip latest back to the last known-good version.
+  npm dist-tag add bmad-module-skill-forge@<previous_good> latest
+
+  # Verify the flip landed.
+  npm dist-tag ls bmad-module-skill-forge
+
+  # Warn anyone who did install <bad> in the meantime.
+  npm deprecate bmad-module-skill-forge@<bad> "Pulled - use <previous_good> instead"
+
+  # Cut the fix forward via the canonical workflow (OIDC publish, SLSA-L2 provenance).
+  gh workflow run release.yaml -f version_bump=patch
+  ```
+
+- **Expected outcome.** `latest` now resolves to `<previous_good>`; `npm install bmad-module-skill-forge@<bad>` emits a deprecation warning; the subsequent patch cut advances `latest` to `<next_version>` cleanly.
+- **Constraints.** Valid only while the blast radius is small. If any downstream has already pinned to `<bad>`, Scenario B applies instead — reverting `latest` won't reach those installs.
+- **Verification.**
+
+  ```bash
+  npm view bmad-module-skill-forge dist-tags --json
+  # expected: {"latest":"<previous_good>", ... "alpha":"0.10.1-alpha.0"}
+  ```
+
+### Scenario B — "Bad version live for hours/days, users may be installing it"
+
+- **Trigger.** Defect discovered after `<bad>` has held `latest` long enough that download count is non-zero, or dependents may have already pinned.
+- **CLI.**
+
+  ```bash
+  # Warn immediately — every install of <bad> now prints this string.
+  npm deprecate bmad-module-skill-forge@<bad> "Critical bug - use <next_version> instead"
+
+  # Cut the fix forward. release.yaml's dist-tag case-chain auto-updates `latest`
+  # for non-prerelease versions, so no manual `dist-tag add` is needed here.
+  gh workflow run release.yaml -f version_bump=patch
+  ```
+
+- **Expected outcome.** `npm install bmad-module-skill-forge` starts emitting the deprecation warning; the new patch publishes cleanly; `latest` advances to `<next_version>`.
+- **Constraints.** **Do NOT** `npm dist-tag add @<previous_good> latest` in this scenario. For post-v1.0.0 releases that is an NFR6 violation (it re-exposes a deprecated version as `latest`); for pre-v1.0.0 it fragments user expectation and hides the fix. Ship forward via patch bump.
+- **Verification.**
+
+  ```bash
+  # Deprecation warning appears on a fresh install.
+  npm install bmad-module-skill-forge@<bad> 2>&1 | grep -i deprecat
+  ```
+
+  NFR3 (rollback frequency <1/quarter post-v1.0.0) is evaluated against the frequency of Scenario B invocations — each trip through Scenario B is an NFR3 data point.
+
+### Scenario C — "Bad version within last 72h, zero downloads, zero dependents"
+
+- **Trigger.** A narrow eligibility window per the [npm unpublish policy](https://docs.npmjs.com/policies/unpublish/): less than 72 hours since publish **AND** zero downloads **AND** zero dependent packages registered on npmjs.com. All three conditions must be true.
+- **CLI.**
+
+  ```bash
+  npm unpublish bmad-module-skill-forge@<bad>
+  ```
+
+- **Expected outcome.** The version disappears from `npm view`. **But** the version *number* is permanently burned — npm rejects any future publish of that same version string forever.
+
+  > **Permanent-burn warning.** The version number is burned. After `npm unpublish bmad-module-skill-forge@0.10.1`, the next `release.yaml` dispatch with `version_bump: patch` on `0.10.0` will produce `0.10.2`, **not** `0.10.1` again. Attempting `npm version 0.10.1 --no-git-tag-version` would drive the workflow's `npm publish` toward an npm-burned version and return `403`. Treat the unpublish as a one-way jump in the version number line, not a true undo.
+
+- **Constraints.** **This scenario does NOT apply to v1.0.0.** Per NFR6, v1.0.0 is never unpublished regardless of eligibility window — use Scenario B instead. Scenario C is a pre-v1.0.0-only path.
+- **Verification.**
+
+  ```bash
+  npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@<bad> 2>&1
+  # expected: npm error 404 No match found for version <bad>
+  ```
+
+### Scenario D — "Tag exists but npm publish failed"
+
+- **Trigger.** `release.yaml` pushed the `v*` tag (tag-creation step runs before the npm publish step) and then the publish step failed — OIDC 404, tarball validation error, network drop. `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@<version>` returns 404; `git tag -l v<version>` shows the tag locally and on origin.
+- **CLI.**
+
+  ```bash
+  # VERIFY FIRST that npm did NOT publish — a tag-delete after a successful
+  # publish would orphan the npm artifact. If this returns a manifest, STOP
+  # and go to Scenario E.
+  npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@<version> 2>&1
+
+  # Tag-only recovery: clear the tag locally and on origin, then re-dispatch.
+  git tag -d v<version>
+  git push --delete origin v<version>
+
+  # Re-dispatch with the same bump input — release.yaml will produce a fresh
+  # clean tag and successful publish.
+  gh workflow run release.yaml -f version_bump=<same-input-as-before>
+  ```
+
+- **Expected outcome.** Tag cleared from origin; re-run produces a fresh clean tag plus a successful publish under the next version number.
+- **Constraints.** Only safe if publish failed. If npm *did* publish, use Scenario E — tag-deletion after a successful publish leaves the npm artifact without a matching git ref.
+- **Do-NOT clause.** Never re-use the burned `<version>` number. `release.yaml` will produce the next version on redispatch (for example, re-running an `alpha` bump over `0.10.1-alpha.0` produces `0.10.1-alpha.1`, not `0.10.1-alpha.0` again). Forcing the original version back via `npm version <exact> --no-git-tag-version` is out of scope for rollback.
+- **Story 3.2 load-bearing context.** The current `release.yaml` pushes the git tag **before** the npm publish step (see the `Create and push tag` job step vs. the `Publish to npm via OIDC trusted publishing` step). This ordering is pre-existing from Story 3.1 and tracked in `_bmad-output/implementation-artifacts/deferred-work.md` under `§ 3-1/3-2 code review "Create and push tag pushes the git tag BEFORE Publish to npm"` as a post-v1.0.0 hardening candidate. Until that lands, Scenario D's tag-delete path **is** the recovery.
+- **Verification.**
+
+  ```bash
+  # Tag gone on origin.
+  gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/git/refs/tags/v<version> 2>&1
+  # expected: 404 Not Found
+  ```
+
+### Scenario E — "npm publish succeeded but GitHub Release / tag push failed"
+
+- **Trigger.** Publish succeeded (`npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@<version>` returns a manifest) but **one of**: tag-push to origin failed (network drop, branch-protection edge case); the `Create GitHub Release` softprops step failed (API rate limit, auth expiry); the `Push commit to main` step was (correctly) skipped but the release artifact diverged from `main`.
+- **Premise inversion note.** In the research doc's scenario ordering, publish was assumed to happen before tag push — so publish failure was the likely branch. In the live `release.yaml` the ordering is reversed: the tag push runs **before** the npm publish. That means a publish-succeeded-but-tag-missing window is small, but real — the `Create GitHub Release` and `Push commit to main` steps still run after publish and either can fail independently. This scenario covers the post-publish-tag-or-release-missing recovery path.
+- **CLI — tag recovery.**
+
+  ```bash
+  # Obtain the commit-sha the workflow used as its HEAD during the publish run.
+  gh run view <run-id> --log | grep -A 2 "release: bump to v<version>"
+
+  # Recreate the annotated tag and push.
+  git tag -a v<version> <commit-sha> -m "Release v<version>"
+  git push origin v<version>
+  ```
+
+- **CLI — GitHub Release recovery.**
+
+  ```bash
+  # Re-create the GitHub Release from the notes the workflow generated
+  # (scrape from the run log if release_notes.md was not preserved as an artifact).
+  gh release create v<version> \
+    --notes-file release_notes.md \
+    --title "Skill Forge (SKF) v<version>"
+  ```
+
+- **Expected outcome.** Tag landed on origin pointing at the correct commit; GitHub Release page reflects the published npm artifact; npm + GitHub + git state are now consistent.
+- **Constraints.** **npm state is immutable.** Do NOT try to "clean up" the npm artifact so the release can be re-run from scratch. The npm artifact plus the orphaned post-recovery git state **is** the canonical record — NFR5 (audit-trail completeness) is satisfied by the successful npm publish plus the recovered tag and GitHub Release, not by a clean rerun.
+- **Orphaned-commit caveat (Story 3.2 context).** If the run was dispatched from a feature branch and `Push commit to main` was correctly skipped, the `release: bump to v<version>` commit lives only in the workflow run log until the recovered tag above anchors it. This is NFR12-compliant and expected pre-v1.0.0 behavior; the tag is the authoritative pointer. This is exactly the pattern observed in the Story 3.2 alpha cut (`bmad-module-skill-forge@0.10.1-alpha.0`, run `24714953668`, tag `v0.10.1-alpha.0`, orphaned commit `2a57dcbd`).
+- **Verification.**
+
+  ```bash
+  # Tag resolves.
+  gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/git/refs/tags/v<version>
+
+  # GitHub Release exists.
+  gh release view v<version>
+
+  # npm + GitHub provenance still match.
+  npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@<version> --json | jq '.dist.attestations'
+  ```
+
+### Scenario F — "Suspected OIDC compromise / unauthorized publish"
+
+- **Trigger (any of).**
+  - `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge time` shows a publish with a timestamp no maintainer triggered.
+  - `npm audit signatures` on a published tarball reports attestation verification failure.
+  - A GitHub Actions run shows reviewer approval that was not given.
+  - npm support notifies of suspected compromise.
+- **Immediate CLI (within minutes of detection).** Revoke the OIDC trust surface before doing anything else:
+  - Visit `https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge` → **Settings** tab → **Trusted Publisher** section → **Delete** the registration.
+  - This blocks *all* future OIDC publishes until re-registered, stopping any further unauthorized use of the OIDC path mid-incident.
+- **Audit CLI.**
+
+  ```bash
+  # Full publish timeline.
+  npm view bmad-module-skill-forge time
+
+  # Per-version provenance shape (script-friendly).
+  npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@<suspect> --json \
+    | jq '.dist.attestations'
+  # expected shape:
+  # {
+  #   "url": "https://registry.npmjs.org/-/npm/v1/attestations/bmad-module-skill-forge@<suspect>",
+  #   "provenance": { "predicateType": "https://slsa.dev/provenance/v1" }
+  # }
+
+  # Human-friendly signature-chain check on a fresh install.
+  npm audit signatures
+  # expected: "X packages have verified attestations"
+  ```
+
+  If `dist.attestations` is missing or the `url` field points somewhere unexpected, the publish bypassed the OIDC path.
+
+- **Coordination CLI.** If a malicious version was actually published, contact `support@npmjs.com` with the version string, the attestation URL, and the Trusted Publisher registration timestamp; request a manual takedown. npm support can unpublish outside the 72-hour window for security-compromise cases.
+- **Lockdown CLI.**
+
+  ```bash
+  # Confirm no unexpected secrets were added to the repo.
+  gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/actions/secrets
+
+  # Confirm the write-access list is unchanged.
+  gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/collaborators
+
+  # Review all workflows for any pull_request trigger type that could run
+  # attacker-controlled code with repo secrets (e.g. `types: [opened]`
+  # without a trusted-author gate).
+  grep -rn "pull_request:" .github/workflows/
+  ```
+
+- **Post-incident reactivation.** After audit completes and root cause is identified and patched, re-register the Trusted Publisher via the npm UI with the four fields matching the table in `## npm Trusted Publisher` above (`organization=armelhbobdad`, `repository=bmad-module-skill-forge`, `workflow filename=release.yaml`, `environment=release`). Re-verify via an alpha cut (dispatch `release.yaml` from a temporarily-allowed feature branch per the `## Release Environment § Temporarily allowing a feature branch` procedure) before any stable release.
+- **Pre-v1.0.0 context.** Trusted Publisher was pre-registered on 2026-04-20 (Story 1.3). A compromise discovered pre-v1.0.0 is recoverable via delete-and-re-register with no downstream-consumer blast radius (no stable release users yet). Post-v1.0.0, the incident has downstream blast radius and the `support@npmjs.com` coordination path is load-bearing.
+- **Do-NOT clause — `NPM_TOKEN` rotation is not OIDC incident response.** Do NOT rotate `NPM_TOKEN` as a first response. The token is not on the OIDC path; rotating it does nothing to stop an OIDC compromise.
+  - If `NPM_TOKEN` itself is *also* suspected compromised, revoke via `https://www.npmjs.com` → **Access Tokens** and remove from the repo: `gh secret delete NPM_TOKEN --repo armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge`. This is separate from the OIDC incident response above.
+  - `NPM_TOKEN` still exists at repo scope until Story 6.3 (post-v1.0.0). A compromised `NPM_TOKEN` is a **separate incident class** from OIDC compromise. During the window where OIDC is revoked *and* `NPM_TOKEN` is also compromised, there is no valid publish path — the repo enters lockdown until Trusted Publisher is re-registered. Document the flip in the incident post-mortem; do not publish via any stale path.
+
+### Scenario G — "release.yaml disabled, reverted, or missing from main"
+
+- **Trigger.** `release.yaml` is the single-rooted release workflow (Story 3.3 Patch A neutralized the legacy `publish.yaml` `v*` tag trigger). If `release.yaml` is reverted, renamed, moved, or disabled at the repo-settings level (`gh workflow disable release.yaml`) and someone tries to cut a release, **no workflow fires**. Silent no-op until a maintainer checks `npm view` or the Actions UI.
+- **Detection.** After any PR that touches `.github/workflows/*` — and before any release attempt — run:
+
+  ```bash
+  gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/actions/workflows \
+    --jq '.workflows[] | select(.name=="Release") | {name, state, path}'
+  # expected: {"name":"Release","state":"active","path":".github/workflows/release.yaml"}
+  ```
+
+  If `state` is not `active` (npm reports `disabled_manually` when disabled via the UI or `gh workflow disable`), or no matching workflow is returned at all (file renamed, moved, or deleted), Scenario G applies.
+
+- **Recovery CLI.**
+
+  ```bash
+  # Case 1: workflow is disabled but the file is present.
+  gh workflow enable release.yaml
+
+  # Case 2: the file is missing or reverted.
+  # Revert the offending PR via the normal branch-protection review path.
+  # Do NOT hand-edit main — branch protection blocks direct push anyway.
+  gh pr view <offending-pr-number>
+  gh pr revert <offending-pr-number>
+  ```
+
+- **Expected outcome.** `release.yaml` is back on `main` with `state: "active"`; next dispatch fires normally.
+- **Prevention.** Single-root invariants to spot-check after any workflow-dir PR:
+
+  ```bash
+  # Any workflow with id-token: write should be a known release/provenance workflow.
+  grep -l 'id-token: write' .github/workflows/*.yaml
+  # expected set: docs.yaml (GitHub Pages), publish.yaml (DEPRECATED, workflow_dispatch-only),
+  # release.yaml (canonical).
+
+  # No v* push trigger outside release.yaml — and release.yaml itself is workflow_dispatch-only,
+  # so there should be zero matches for `v*` push triggers anywhere.
+  grep -rE 'tags:\s*$|v\*' .github/workflows/*.yaml
+  ```
+
+- **Escalation path — emergency hatch.** If Scenario G is detected mid-incident when a release is urgently needed, the legacy `.github/workflows/publish.yaml` retains `workflow_dispatch:` as an emergency hatch (NPM_TOKEN still at repo scope until Story 6.3). Invoke via:
+
+  ```bash
+  gh workflow run publish.yaml
+  ```
+
+  with the explicit understanding that this publish is **token-based (not OIDC)** and will **not** carry SLSA-L2 provenance. Document every emergency-hatch use in an incident post-mortem, and re-enable `release.yaml` before the next release. This fallback disappears when Story 6.1 deletes `publish.yaml`.
+
+- **Constraints.** Branch protection on `main` blocks direct pushes — recovery goes through a PR in every case. Do not attempt to sidestep branch protection to "fix" `release.yaml` faster; the cost of a bad release (NFR5 audit-trail breakage, NFR10 commit-trail breakage) far exceeds the cost of a normal-review PR.
+
+### Cross-reference matrix
+
+| Scenario | Trigger class                   | Primary CLI verb              | NFR linkage           |
+| -------- | ------------------------------- | ----------------------------- | --------------------- |
+| A        | Fresh bad latest                | `dist-tag` + `deprecate`      | NFR3                  |
+| B        | Stale bad latest                | `deprecate` + ship forward    | NFR3, NFR6            |
+| C        | Eligible unpublish              | `unpublish`                   | (pre-v1.0.0 only)     |
+| D        | Tag orphan (publish failed)     | `tag -d` + `push --delete`    | NFR12                 |
+| E        | Post-publish state drift        | `tag -a` + `gh release create`| NFR5                  |
+| F        | OIDC compromise                 | revoke Trusted Publisher + audit | NFR7               |
+| G        | `release.yaml` disabled/missing | `workflow enable` / `pr revert` | NFR5, NFR10         |
+
+### Baseline snapshots
+
+The ruleset-restore snippet at the top of this document (`## Branch Protection on main § Restore from a saved baseline`) uses `gh api --method PUT .../rulesets/<id>`. That call returns `404` if the `Default` ruleset has been deleted, not merely edited. Keeping a recent `baseline-ruleset-Default-YYYYMMDD.json` on disk lets the PUT-then-POST fallback below recover from either case.
+
+**One-time capture pattern** — run after any maintainer-initiated change to the ruleset or to the `release` environment, **not on a schedule**:
+
+```bash
+# Capture the current Default ruleset as a disaster-recovery baseline.
+# Uses name-based lookup so the snippet survives ruleset re-creation (IDs change, names don't).
+RULESET_ID=$(gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/rulesets \
+  --jq '.[] | select(.name=="Default") | .id')
+gh api "repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/rulesets/$RULESET_ID" \
+  > "_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/baseline-ruleset-Default-$(date +%Y%m%d).json"
+
+# Capture the release environment + its branch policy list (two separate resources).
+gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release \
+  > "_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/baseline-env-release-$(date +%Y%m%d).json"
+gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies \
+  > "_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/baseline-env-release-branch-policies-$(date +%Y%m%d).json"
+```
+
+Baselines live in `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/` (already git-tracked, not shipped with the npm package). The filename date stamp makes the freshness of the baseline obvious at a glance.
+
+**Ruleset-deletion recovery path — PUT-then-POST.** The canonical restore flow is:
+
+1. Try `PUT` first. If the ruleset exists but drifted, this reconciles it in place.
+
+   ```bash
+   RULESET_ID=$(gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/rulesets \
+     --jq '.[] | select(.name=="Default") | .id')
+   jq '{name, target, enforcement, conditions, rules, bypass_actors}' \
+     _bmad-output/planning-artifacts/baseline-ruleset-Default-YYYYMMDD.json \
+     > /tmp/restore.json
+   gh api --method PUT \
+     "repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/rulesets/$RULESET_ID" \
+     --input /tmp/restore.json
+   ```
+
+2. If the `PUT` returns `404` (ruleset was deleted, not edited), recreate via `POST`:
+
+   ```bash
+   gh api --method POST \
+     repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/rulesets \
+     --input /tmp/restore.json
+   ```
+
+The `POST` body needs the same `name`, `target`, `enforcement`, `conditions`, `rules`, and `bypass_actors` fields as the PUT — all captured by the baseline snapshot above. Try `PUT` first; on `404`, `POST` recreates with identical semantics. The ruleset gets a new id on re-creation (IDs are not stable across delete+create cycles); update any hard-coded id references in this document on the next planned edit pass.
+
+For the `release` environment, a deletion+restore similarly uses the two-call pattern already documented at `## Release Environment § Restore / re-apply` — feed the environment-level baseline JSON to the `PUT .../environments/release` call, then re-POST each entry from the branch-policies baseline to `.../environments/release/deployment-branch-policies`.

file.content:
# DEPRECATED: Replaced by release.yaml (see docs/RELEASING.md). Scheduled for removal post-v1.0.0 per Epic 6. Do NOT invoke.
name: Publish to NPM

on:
  workflow_dispatch:

permissions:
  contents: read
  id-token: write # Required for OIDC

jobs:
  publish:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Setup Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version-file: ".nvmrc"
          registry-url: "https://registry.npmjs.org"

      - name: Install uv
        uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v6
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: Run full test suite
        run: npm test

      - name: Publish to NPM
        run: npm publish --provenance
        env:
          NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}

content.text:
Verified key facts. Now triaging.

**Critical fact-checks done:**
- `gh pr revert` **does exist** (gh ≥2.91); it opens a revert PR — Blind Hunter's "Critical: doesn't exist" is **wrong**, but Edge Hunter's "doesn't auto-merge" is **right**.
- `dist.attestations` is **an object**, not an array (verified live against `0.10.0`) — Blind Hunter's claim is **wrong**; the diff's expected shape is correct.
- `publish.yaml:36` actually does call `npm publish --provenance` with `id-token: write` — the playbook's "will not carry SLSA-L2 provenance" claim is **misleading**: provenance attestation rides on the GitHub OIDC token (independent of trusted-publisher auth). The legacy hatch likely WILL emit a provenance attestation.
- `.github/workflows/skill-review.yml` (the `.yml` glob omission Edge #14 flagged) — confirmed real omission target.
- "npm reports `disabled_manually`" at `docs/RELEASING.md:+246` — confirmed wrong subject; should be "GitHub Actions API reports".

Now reading step-04 and presenting.

content.content: 1 --- 2 deferred_work_file: '{implementation_artifacts}/deferred-work.md' 3 --- 4 5 # Step 4: Present and Act 6 7 ## RULES 8 9 - YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}` 10 - When `{spec_file}` is set, always write findings to the story file before offering action choices. 11 - `decision-needed` findings must be resolved before handling `patch` findings. 12 13 ## INSTRUCTIONS 14 15 ### 1. Clean review shortcut 16 17 If zero findings remain after triage (all dismissed or none raised): state that and proceed to section 6 (Sprint Status Update). 18 19 ### 2. Write findings to the story file 20 21 If `{spec_file}` exists and contains a Tasks/Subtasks section, append a `### Review Findings` subsection. Write all findings in this order: 22 23 1. **`decision-needed`** findings (unchecked): 24 `- [ ] [Review][Decision] — <Detail>` 25 26 2. **`patch`** findings (unchecked): 27 `- [ ] [Review][Patch] <Title> [<file>:<line>]` 28 29 3. **`defer`** findings (checked off, marked deferred): 30 `- [x] [Review][Defer] <Title> [<file>:<line>] — deferred, pre-existing` 31 32 Also append each `defer` finding to `{deferred_work_file}` under a heading `## Deferred from: code review ({date})`. If `{spec_file}` is set, include its basename in the heading (e.g., `code review of story-3.3 (2026-03-18)`). One bullet per finding with description. 33 34 ### 3. Present summary 35 36 Announce what was written: 37 38 > **Code review complete.** <D> `decision-needed`, <P> `patch`, <W> `defer`, <R> dismissed as noise. 39 40 If `{spec_file}` is set, add: `Findings written to the review findings section in {spec_file}.` 41 Otherwise add: `Findings are listed above. No story file was provided, so nothing was persisted.` 42 43 ### 4. Resolve decision-needed findings 44 45 If `decision_needed` findings exist, present each one with its detail and the options available. The user must decide — the correct fix is ambiguous without their input. Walk through each finding (or batch related ones) and get the user's call. Once resolved, each becomes a `patch`, `defer`, or is dismissed. 46 47 If the user chooses to defer, ask: Quick one-line reason for deferring this item? (helps future reviews): — then append that reason to both the story file bullet and the `{deferred_work_file}` entry. 48 49 **HALT** — I am waiting for your numbered choice. Reply with only the number (or "0" for batch). Do not proceed until you select an option. 50 51 ### 5. Handle `patch` findings 52 53 If `patch` findings exist (including any resolved from step 4), HALT. Ask the user: 54 55 If `{spec_file}` is set, present all three options (if >3 `patch` findings exist, also show option 0): 56 57 > **How would you like to handle the <Z> `patch` findings?** 58 > 0. **Batch-apply all** — automatically fix every non-controversial patch (recommended when there are many) 59 > 1. **Fix them automatically** — I will apply fixes now 60 > 2. **Leave as action items** — they are already in the story file 61 > 3. **Walk through each** — let me show details before deciding 62 63 If `{spec_file}` is **not** set, present only options 1 and 3 (omit option 2 — findings were not written to a file). If >3 `patch` findings exist, also show option 0: 64 65 > **How would you like to handle the <Z> `patch` findings?** 66 > 0. **Batch-apply all** — automatically fix every non-controversial patch (recommended when there are many) 67 > 1. **Fix them automatically** — I will apply fixes now 68 > 2. **Walk through each** — let me show details before deciding 69 70 **HALT** — I am waiting for your numbered choice. Reply with only the number (or "0" for batch). Do not proceed until you select an option. 71 72 - **Option 0** (only when >3 findings): Apply all non-controversial patches without per-finding confirmation. Skip any finding that requires judgment. Present a summary of changes made and any skipped findings. 73 - **Option 1**: Apply each fix. After all patches are applied, present a summary of changes made. If `{spec_file}` is set, check off the items in the story file. 74 - **Option 2** (only when `{spec_file}` is set): Done — findings are already written to the story. 75 - **Walk through each**: Present each finding with full detail, diff context, and suggested fix. After walkthrough, re-offer the applicable options above. 76 77 **HALT** — I am waiting for your numbered choice. Reply with only the number (or "0" for batch). Do not proceed until you select an option. 78 79 **✅ Code review actions complete** 80 81 - Decision-needed resolved: <D> 82 - Patches handled: <P> 83 - Deferred: <W> 84 - Dismissed: <R> 85 86 ### 6. Update story status and sync sprint tracking 87 88 Skip this section if `{spec_file}` is not set. 89 90 #### Determine new status based on review outcome 91 92 - If all `decision-needed` and `patch` findings were resolved (fixed or dismissed) AND no unresolved HIGH/MEDIUM issues remain: set `{new_status}` = `done`. Update the story file Status section to `done`. 93 - If `patch` findings were left as action items, or unresolved issues remain: set `{new_status}` = `in-progress`. Update the story file Status section to `in-progress`. 94 95 Save the story file. 96 97 #### Sync sprint-status.yaml 98 99 If `{story_key}` is not set, skip this subsection and note that sprint status was not synced because no story key was available. 100 101 If `{sprint_status}` file exists: 102 103 1. Load the FULL `{sprint_status}` file. 104 2. Find the `development_status` entry matching `{story_key}`. 105 3. If found: update `development_status[{story_key}]` to `{new_status}`. Update `last_updated` to current date. Save the file, preserving ALL comments and structure including STATUS DEFINITIONS. 106 4. If `{story_key}` not found in sprint status: warn the user that the story file was updated but sprint-status sync failed. 107 108 If `{sprint_status}` file does not exist, note that story status was updated in the story file only. 109 110 #### Completion summary 111 112 > **Review Complete!** 113 > 114 > **Story Status:** `{new_status}` 115 > **Issues Fixed:** <fixed_count> 116 > **Action Items Created:** <action_count> 117 > **Deferred:** <W> 118 > **Dismissed:** <R> 119 120 ### 7. Next steps 121 122 Present the user with follow-up options: 123 124 > **What would you like to do next?** 125 > 1. **Start the next story** — run `dev-story` to pick up the next `ready-for-dev` story 126 > 2. **Re-run code review** — address findings and review again 127 > 3. **Done** — end the workflow 128 129 **HALT** — I am waiting for your choice. Do not proceed until the user selects an option. 130

content.content:
80	- **Git tag pushed before npm publish — OIDC failure orphans the tag on `origin`** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:161-167` (`Create and push tag`) vs `:181` (`Publish to npm via OIDC trusted publishing`). Pre-existing from Story 3.1; Story 3.2 does not introduce but now the `--tag` derivation change advertises a dry-run gate that (correctly) does NOT cover OIDC failures. If OIDC handshake fails after tag-push, `v$VERSION` exists on GitHub without an npm artifact. Belongs with Story 4.1 rollback playbook or a dedicated hardening story (reorder to tag-after-publish, or add a compensating tag-delete step on publish failure).
81	- **Feature-branch alpha-cut tag commits are orphaned on branch deletion** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:167` (unconditional `git push origin "v..."`) vs `:170` (conditional `Push commit to main` gated on `github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'`). On `workflow_dispatch` from a feature branch (Story 3.2 alpha-cut pattern), the `v0.10.1-alpha.0` tag points to a commit that exists only on the feature branch. Post-merge branch deletion may GC that commit depending on GitHub retention; `git describe --tags --abbrev=0` in later runs may fail or pick the wrong previous tag. Interacts with Story 4.1 rollback semantics and with FR / NFR around release-history lineage. Track with Story 3.3 or Story 4.1.
82	- **Execution-quality note — each failed run required a fresh user-approval click at the env gate.** In a solo-maintainer context (`prevent_self_review: false`), this costs ~5–10s of maintainer time per retry. In a post-Epic-1-Carry-forward-#3 world (second reviewer onboarded, `prevent_self_review: true` flipped), each failed run would require the second reviewer's re-approval — materially increasing iteration cost on workflow defects. NFR1 (15-min CI cycle) re-evaluation for Story 5.1 should account for this. Not a workflow defect; a real-world cost to surface for audit.
83	
84	## Deferred from: code review of 3-3-merge-release-yaml-to-main-and-retain-legacy-workflows-as-inert-backup (2026-04-21)
85	
86	- **Non-numeric release version headers bypass section 1→2 transition** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:139`. The awk rule `section==1 && /^## \[[0-9]/` only transitions on `[<digit>`, so hypothetical future tag schemes like `## [v0.11.0]` (with a `v` prefix) or `## [rc-1]` (no leading digit) would be absorbed into the preamble accumulator and the new release would be misplaced. Current SemVer convention always starts with a digit (including pre-release identifiers like `0.10.1-alpha.0`), so this does not fire today. Robustness-only; broaden to `/^## \[/ && !/^## \[Unreleased\]/` when a future story touches this step.
87	- **Zero-conventional-commit release path — workflow proceeds with empty changelog entry** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:119` (Update CHANGELOG.md step). If there are no conventional commits since the last tag, `conventional-changelog-cli -s` prepends nothing; the preamble-restore guard correctly no-ops, but the workflow continues to tag + publish with no CHANGELOG entry for the new version. Upstream concern (belongs to Update CHANGELOG, not Restore Preamble); candidate for an Epic 4 release-safety hardening pass — detect empty prepend and `exit 1`.
88	- **Duplicate `# Changelog` headings in malformed input produce duplicated preamble output** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:138`. Rare pre-existing data-corruption scenario (e.g., from a botched merge): the first `# Changelog` line transitions 0→1 correctly; any additional `# Changelog` lines are swept into the preamble accumulator and duplicated in the output. Not introduced by Patch C — any reorder against that input shape is ambiguous. Out of scope until we see one in the wild.
89	- **No post-restore diff/sanity-check step for CI debuggability** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:149`. Purely diagnostic — a `head -20 CHANGELOG.md` or `git diff CHANGELOG.md | head` after the restore would speed root-causing if the `Assert no bogus issue refs` step ever fails on a mutated file. Not a correctness issue. Bundle with any future release-workflow debugging pass.
90	- **No end-to-end assertion that `release.yaml` owns the `v*` tag-push path now that `publish.yaml` does not** — `.github/workflows/publish.yaml:4-5` removed `push: tags: - "v*"` (neutralized per Patch A). If `release.yaml` is ever disabled, deleted, or reverted, a `v*` tag push would now silently no-op (no publish, no GitHub Release, no tag-to-artifact linkage). Belongs to Epic 4's RELEASING.md six-scenario rollback playbook — the "release.yaml disabled" scenario needs a runbook entry and monitoring signal. Low probability, high-consequence fallback gap.
91

input.old_string:
## Dev Notes

### What this story actually is
input.new_string:
### Review Findings

_Appended by `bmad-code-review` on 2026-04-23. Three reviewers: Blind Hunter (diff-only), Edge Case Hunter (diff + project), Acceptance Auditor (diff + spec). Acceptance Auditor returned 20 PASS / 0 FAIL / 2 N/A — no AC violations. The patch list below is correctness, ergonomics, and shell-safety findings on the new prose._

**Patch (12) — to fix in Story 4.1 close-out:**

- [ ] [Review][Patch] Scenario E commit-SHA recovery CLI doesn't return a SHA [docs/RELEASING.md:+148] — `gh run view <run-id> --log | grep -A 2 "release: bump to v<version>"` matches the `git commit -m` echo line; the SHA appears further down on the `[<branch> <sha>] release: bump...` confirmation line. Edge Hunter live-tested against run `24714953668` and confirmed nothing usable returns. Replace with `gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/actions/runs/<run-id> --jq .head_sha` (one-line, returns the SHA directly).
- [ ] [Review][Patch] "npm reports `disabled_manually`" is the wrong subject [docs/RELEASING.md:+246] — `disabled_manually` is a GitHub Actions API state. A responder unfamiliar with the API will search npm docs / `npm view` for the term and lose time mid-incident. Replace "npm reports" with "the GitHub Actions API reports".
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Emergency-hatch description is misleading on two factual points [docs/RELEASING.md:+275-281] — (a) `publish.yaml:36` actually invokes `npm publish --provenance` with `id-token: write` permission, so npm WILL emit a SLSA-L2 provenance attestation via the GitHub OIDC token (independent of the trusted-publisher OIDC auth path). The "will not carry SLSA-L2 provenance" claim is false. (b) `publish.yaml` does NOT bump version, push the `v*` tag, update CHANGELOG, or create a GitHub Release — every emergency-hatch invocation leaves npm + git state diverged and requires a Scenario-E-style reconciliation afterward. Neither is mentioned. Rewrite to: emit-provenance-yes-OIDC-trust-no, plus a "post-hatch reconciliation: bump `package.json`, recreate tag + GitHub Release per Scenario E" follow-up note.
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Scenario G prevention `*.yaml` glob misses `.yml` files [docs/RELEASING.md:+266, +272] — `.github/workflows/skill-review.yml` exists today; future `.yml` workflows with `id-token: write` would not be flagged by `grep -l 'id-token: write' .github/workflows/*.yaml`. Use `.github/workflows/*.{yaml,yml}` (bash brace expansion) or `find .github/workflows -name '*.y*ml'`.
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Scenario G `gh pr revert` opens a PR but doesn't merge it [docs/RELEASING.md:+258] — verb exists (gh ≥ 2.91), but it creates a draftable revert PR that still requires branch-protection review. Recovery dead-ends if the responder treats it as one-shot. Add a follow-up `gh pr merge <revert-pr> --squash --auto` step or call out explicitly that this opens a PR which must be approved and merged before retry.
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Scenario A verification mixes placeholder with literal [docs/RELEASING.md:+59] — `# expected: {"latest":"<previous_good>", ... "alpha":"0.10.1-alpha.0"}` mixes substitute-me (`<previous_good>`) with hard-coded literal (`0.10.1-alpha.0`). Reader can't tell which fields are illustrative vs authoritative; the alpha tag's existence is asserted with no setup in the playbook. Make both placeholder (`"alpha":"<latest_alpha>"`) or drop the alpha line.
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Scenario B verification has two issues [docs/RELEASING.md:+82] — (a) `npm install bmad-module-skill-forge@<bad> 2>&1 | grep -i deprecat` matches transitive-dep deprecation warnings, not the message specifically; (b) `npm install` mutates the cwd's `package.json` / `package-lock.json` if run in any Node project (e.g., the SKF repo root). Replace with `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@<bad> deprecated` — no install needed, returns the exact deprecation string from the registry.
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Scenario E recovery references `release_notes.md` that may not exist [docs/RELEASING.md:+158-163] — the `# (scrape from the run log if release_notes.md was not preserved as an artifact)` parenthetical admits this, but the example `gh release create v<version> --notes-file release_notes.md` runs unconditionally. A responder hits `release_notes.md: No such file or directory`. Add `--generate-notes` as a fallback flag, or document the explicit "scrape from log first → write to release_notes.md → run gh release create" sequence.
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Scenario F `npm audit signatures` returns clean if cwd has no install [docs/RELEASING.md:+207] — running in an empty incident-response directory returns `0 packages have verified attestations` — false-clean signal mid-incident. Wrap with `mkdir /tmp/skf-verify && cd /tmp/skf-verify && npm init -y && npm install bmad-module-skill-forge@<suspect> --ignore-scripts` first, OR document explicitly that the command operates on the cwd dependency tree.
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Scenario G prevention regex is overly broad [docs/RELEASING.md:+272] — `grep -rE 'tags:\s*$|v\*' .github/workflows/*.yaml` — `tags:\s*$` matches any line ending in `tags:` (including outputs / job-output blocks); `v\*` matches literal `v*` anywhere (in comments, docstrings, etc.). Tighten to specifically catch `push: tags:` blocks containing `v*` patterns, e.g. `grep -rE -A2 '^\s*push:' .github/workflows/*.{yaml,yml} | grep -E "['\"]v\*"`.
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Scenario G `id-token: write` allowlist contradicts deprecation narrative [docs/RELEASING.md:+267] — listing DEPRECATED `publish.yaml` as part of the "expected set" for a security-sensitive grep contradicts the rest of the doc treating `publish.yaml` as legacy/inert. A reviewer running this check during incident response can't tell whether `publish.yaml`-in-the-list is fine ("expected") or a finding ("DEPRECATED, why is this still here?"). Add a "(DEPRECATED — Story 6.1 deletes it; until then it's expected)" parenthetical to the `publish.yaml` entry.
- [ ] [Review][Patch] `baseline-ruleset-Default-YYYYMMDD.json` literal in shell snippet [docs/RELEASING.md:+328] — appears inside an executable `jq` command, but `YYYYMMDD` is a substitution token, not a real date. Bash will read it as a literal filename and the `jq` command will fail with `no such file`. Use `<YYYYMMDD>` to match the rest of the doc's `<placeholder>` convention, or substitute a glob like `$(ls -t _bmad-output/planning-artifacts/baseline-ruleset-Default-*.json | head -1)`.

**Defer (8) — pre-existing or scope-expansion; tracked in `deferred-work.md`:**

- [x] [Review][Defer] Baseline `jq` filter assumes GET shape matches PUT shape [docs/RELEASING.md:+327-332] — deferred, GitHub rulesets API GET vs PUT body shape divergence needs validation outside this review.
- [x] [Review][Defer] Scenario G detection misses ghost-registered workflows after file deletion — deferred, scope expansion beyond AC 9.
- [x] [Review][Defer] Scenario G `gh workflow enable` requires admin permissions — deferred, no second-maintainer ACL gap exists today (single-maintainer repo).
- [x] [Review][Defer] Scenario A → B boundary blurred by npm CDN cache lag — deferred, scope expansion beyond AC 3.
- [x] [Review][Defer] Scenario C 72h boundary uses npm-clock not local-clock — deferred, low-likelihood edge case.
- [x] [Review][Defer] Scenario C "zero downloads" has 24-48h npm stats lag — deferred, scope expansion beyond AC 5; a "treat C as B if in doubt" sentence would suffice but adds prose under pressure.
- [x] [Review][Defer] Scenario D tag-deletion may be blocked by future tag-protection ruleset — deferred, speculative against post-v1.0.0 hardening.
- [x] [Review][Defer] Scenario F UI revoke flow assumes 2FA intact — deferred, npm-support coordination path partially covers this; full account-recovery branching would significantly expand the scenario.

**Dismissed (7) — false positives or noise:** Blind Hunter "`gh pr revert` doesn't exist" (verified — it does, since gh ≥ 2.91); Blind Hunter "`dist.attestations` is array" (verified live against `0.10.0` — it's an object, the diff is correct); Blind Hunter "README 'seven failure scenarios' framing" (count is right); Blind Hunter "Scenario C 404 expected output approximate" (practical enough); Blind Hunter "Scenario D no SHA sanity check" (scope creep); Edge Hunter "Scenario D 404 cache-vs-success distinction" (minor); Edge Hunter deferred-work quote-paraphrase nit (cosmetic).

Edge Hunter live-tested against run `24714953668` and confirmed nothing usable returns. Replace with `gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/actions/runs/<run-id> --jq .head_sha` (one-line, returns the SHA directly).
- [ ] [Review][Patch] "npm reports `disabled_manually`" is the wrong subject [docs/RELEASING.md:+246] — `disabled_manually` is a GitHub Actions API state. A responder unfamiliar with the API will search npm docs / `npm view` for the term and lose time mid-incident. Replace "npm reports" with "the GitHub Actions API reports".
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Emergency-hatch description is misleading on two factual points [docs/RELEASING.md:+275-281] — (a) `publish.yaml:36` actually invokes `npm publish --provenance` with `id-token: write` permission, so npm WILL emit a SLSA-L2 provenance attestation via the GitHub OIDC token (independent of the trusted-publisher OIDC auth path). The "will not carry SLSA-L2 provenance" claim is false. (b) `publish.yaml` does NOT bump version, push the `v*` tag, update CHANGELOG, or create a GitHub Release — every emergency-hatch invocation leaves npm + git state diverged and requires a Scenario-E-style reconciliation afterward. input.description: Inspect end of deferred-work.md before append

content.content:
- **Duplicate `# Changelog` headings in malformed input produce duplicated preamble output** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:138`. Rare pre-existing data-corruption scenario (e.g., from a botched merge): the first `# Changelog` line transitions 0→1 correctly; any additional `# Changelog` lines are swept into the preamble accumulator and duplicated in the output. Not introduced by Patch C — any reorder against that input shape is ambiguous. Out of scope until we see one in the wild.
- **No post-restore diff/sanity-check step for CI debuggability** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:149`. Purely diagnostic — a `head -20 CHANGELOG.md` or `git diff CHANGELOG.md | head` after the restore would speed root-causing if the `Assert no bogus issue refs` step ever fails on a mutated file. Not a correctness issue. Bundle with any future release-workflow debugging pass.
- **No end-to-end assertion that `release.yaml` owns the `v*` tag-push path now that `publish.yaml` does not** — `.github/workflows/publish.yaml:4-5` removed `push: tags: - "v*"` (neutralized per Patch A). If `release.yaml` is ever disabled, deleted, or reverted, a `v*` tag push would now silently no-op (no publish, no GitHub Release, no tag-to-artifact linkage). Belongs to Epic 4's RELEASING.md six-scenario rollback playbook — the "release.yaml disabled" scenario needs a runbook entry and monitoring signal. Low probability, high-consequence fallback gap.

## Deferred from: code review of 4-1-author-docs-releasing-md-with-six-scenario-rollback-playbook (2026-04-23)

- **Baseline `jq` filter assumes GET shape matches PUT shape** — `docs/RELEASING.md:+327-332` (Story 4.1). The `### Baseline snapshots` PUT-then-POST recovery applies a `jq '{name, target, enforcement, conditions, rules, bypass_actors}'` filter to a baseline captured from `GET /repos/.../rulesets/<id>` and feeds it to `PUT .../rulesets/<id>`. GitHub's REST API GET vs PUT body shapes for rulesets can differ subtly (e.g., `bypass_actors` reshape, `conditions` nested differently). A round-trip restore drill is the only way to confirm the snippet works end-to-end. Validate against a deliberate ruleset edit + PUT-restore exercise; update the `jq` filter if the PUT 422s.
- **Scenario G detection misses ghost-registered workflows after file deletion** — `docs/RELEASING.md:+240-244` (Story 4.1). GitHub keeps a workflow `state:"active"` for some time after its file is deleted from the default branch. Edge-Hunter live-verified this on the SKF repo: `env-gate-test.yaml` returns `state:"active"` despite the file being absent. If `release.yaml` is deleted via PR, the same lag applies — the AC 9.b detection passes, the next dispatch fails with `Workflow does not exist`, and the responder concludes the playbook check is reliable when it isn't. Augment with a file-presence check on `main` (e.g., `gh api repos/.../contents/.github/workflows/release.yaml`) or `git ls-tree main -- .github/workflows/release.yaml`. Bundle with any future Scenario G hardening pass.
- **Scenario G `gh workflow enable` requires admin permissions** — `docs/RELEASING.md:+252` (Story 4.1). A responder with `triage` or `maintain` role cannot enable workflows; only `admin` can. SKF is single-maintainer today so this gap is dormant. When a second maintainer onboards (Epic 1 Carry-forward #3 — `prevent_self_review: true`), document the ACL requirement and an escalation path or default to the emergency hatch.
- **Scenario A → B boundary blurred by npm CDN cache lag** — `docs/RELEASING.md:+36, +59` (Story 4.1). `npm view <pkg> dist-tags` reads through a CDN with multi-second to multi-minute eventual consistency. "Still shows `<bad>` on `latest`" is not a reliable "no users yet" signal — installs hit different cache nodes. A maintainer running `npm view` 60s after publish can see `<bad>` on `latest` while CI bots in another region have already pulled from a warmer node and committed lockfiles pinning `<bad>`. Add a freshness cross-check (`npm view ... --json | jq '.time | to_entries | last'`) or an explicit "this is a quick visual check, not authoritative" caveat.
- **Scenario C 72h boundary uses npm-clock, not local-clock** — `docs/RELEASING.md:+89` (Story 4.1). The "less than 72 hours since publish" check should be computed from `npm view <pkg>@<bad> time`, not the responder's local `date`. At the boundary, npm's policy engine uses its server timestamp; a guess off by a few minutes 422s the unpublish. Add a one-line "compute from `npm view ... time`" caveat.
- **Scenario C "zero downloads" has 24-48h npm stats lag** — `docs/RELEASING.md:+89` (Story 4.1). The npm downloads API is documented as eventually consistent with up to 24-48h lag; a published version can show "0 downloads" while having been installed by hundreds of CI runs. Treating `npm view` and the implied download count as authoritative in the first 24-48h post-publish is unsafe. Add explicit warning: "the 'zero downloads' criterion is unreliable in the first 24-48h due to npm's stats lag. When in doubt, treat C as Scenario B."
- **Scenario D tag-deletion may be blocked by future tag-protection ruleset** — `docs/RELEASING.md:+121` (Story 4.1). The Default ruleset's `deletion` rule applies to branches; tag protection is a separate ruleset / rule type. The playbook does not state whether tag deletion on `origin` is currently allowed or blocked. If tag-deletion protection is added in any future PR (or if a `tag` rule is added to the existing ruleset), Scenario D's `git push --delete origin v<version>` silently 422s and the recovery dead-ends. Speculative — no rule today; revisit if a `tag` rule is added.
- **Scenario F UI revoke flow assumes 2FA intact** — `docs/RELEASING.md:+188-190` (Story 4.1). The "Visit npmjs.com → Settings → Trusted Publisher → Delete" path is gated on the maintainer's 2FA. The trigger list explicitly includes "npm support notifies of suspected compromise" — a strong signal that account access may itself be compromised. The first-step revoke fails closed in that case; only the later `support@npmjs.com` coordination path is documented as a fallback. Add an "Immediate CLI — account-locked variant" that routes through `support@npmjs.com` *before* attempting the UI flow if 2FA is degraded; reference npm's account-recovery URL.
